query: Assuming ERCOT solar PPA prices including RECs were $28/MWh, $40/MWh, $45/MWh and $46/MWh from 2021 to 2024, how would merchant revenues for solar assets in ERCOT west compare with these PPA revenues with REC-only prices at $2.5/MWh? Asuume there is no curtailment and solar production is consistnet with overall ERCOT solar production profile
final_answer:
Excellent! Now I have everything I need to write the full, rich analysis. Let me compile the final narrative.

---

## ERCOT West Solar: Merchant Revenue vs. PPA Revenue (2021–2024)

**Methodology & Assumptions:**
- **Solar profile**: ERCOT system-wide real-time solar generation (from `ercot_fuel_mix`, normalized to a 0–1 capacity factor shape) — consistent with "overall ERCOT solar production profile" as requested
- **Energy prices**: ERCOT West Hub real-time LMP (15-min data, hourly-averaged) from `ercot_lmp`
- **Solar location reference**: Midland, TX (31.997°N, 102.078°W) via Renewables.ninja to confirm annual CFs (~25-27%)
- **Merchant revenue** = solar capture price (production-weighted LMP) + $2.50/MWh RECs
- **PPA revenue** = given fixed PPA $/MWh (already inclusive of RECs)
- No curtailment assumed; no degradation charges applied

---

### 📊 TABLE 1: Revenue Per MWh of Generation ($/MWh)

| Metric | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solar Capture Price** (energy only) | $78.78 | $74.69 | $73.59 | $21.95 |
| + REC Revenue | $2.50 | $2.50 | $2.50 | $2.50 |
| **= Merchant Total (energy + RECs)** | **$81.28** | **$77.19** | **$76.09** | **$24.45** |
| | | | | |
| **PPA Price (all-in, incl. RECs)** | **$28.00** | **$40.00** | **$45.00** | **$46.00** |
| | | | | |
| 🔼/🔽 **Delta: Merchant MINUS PPA** | **+$53.28 ✅** | **+$37.19 ✅** | **+$31.09 ✅** | **−$21.55 ❌** |

---

### 📊 TABLE 2: Annual Revenue Per MW Installed ($/MW/year)

| Metric | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Annual CF (%) | 24.9% | 26.9% | 26.6% | 25.1% |
| Annual Generation (MWh/MW) | 2,182 | 2,358 | 2,326 | 2,201 |
| Flat Average ERCOT West LMP ($/MWh) | $145.81 | $57.49 | $49.47 | $27.44 |
| | | | | |
| **Merchant Revenue** (energy + RECs) | **$177,366** | **$182,013** | **$176,977** | **$53,807** |
| **PPA Revenue** (incl. RECs) | $61,102 | $94,322 | $104,666 | $101,249 |
| **Delta: Merchant MINUS PPA** | **+$116,264 ✅** | **+$87,692 ✅** | **+$72,311 ✅** | **−$47,442 ❌** |

---

### 📊 TABLE 3: LMP Distribution During Solar Hours (ERCOT West Hub)

| Metric | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Daytime P10 LMP ($/MWh) | $0.8 | –$0.3 | $0.2 | –$0.3 |
| Daytime P50 LMP ($/MWh) | $26.1 | $43.5 | $21.7 | $18.2 |
| Daytime P90 LMP ($/MWh) | $63.5 | $96.8 | $74.4 | $51.5 |
| Negative-price hours (daytime) | 7.6% | 11.1% | 8.8% | 11.6% |

---

### 🔍 Key Findings & Drivers

#### 1. 🏆 2021 — Massive Merchant Outperformance (+$53/MWh; +$116K/MW)
The **2021 flat average ERCOT West LMP was $145.81/MWh** — the highest in any year — almost entirely due to **Winter Storm Uri (Feb 10-20)**, which drove prices to $9,199/MWh and a 10-day average of $3,868/MWh. Solar plants were running during some Uri daylight hours and captured an average of $3,785/MWh during that period. Even excluding Uri, 2021 solar capture (~$37.58/MWh) still well exceeded the $28 PPA.

> ⚠️ **Important caveat**: The outsized 2021 merchant figure is a **once-in-a-decade event** — a merchant investor capturing these prices in real life would need to have stayed unhedged through Uri. In practice, many contracts and prudency standards may prevent this.

#### 2. ✅ 2022 — Strong Outperformance (+$37/MWh; +$88K/MW) with Counterintuitive Capture
Despite the flat average LMP dropping from $146 to $57, the solar capture rate jumped to **129.9%** (capture price higher than flat average), because the Russia/Ukraine energy crisis pushed gas and power prices up disproportionately during peak demand hours that overlap with solar production. The P90 daytime LMP was $96.8/MWh.

#### 3. ✅ 2023 — Continued Outperformance (+$31/MWh; +$72K/MW), But Narrowing Gap
Capture price remained elevated at $73.59/MWh against a flat average of $49.47/MWh (capture rate: **149%**!). Solar was quite well-correlated with peak prices this year. However, the PPA price of $45 was creeping up toward the capture price, signaling the market was beginning to fairly price long-run solar value.

#### 4. ❌ 2024 — Merchant Cracks Under Solar Cannibalisation (−$21.55/MWh; −$47K/MW)
This is **the canary in the coal mine for Texas solar.** With massive build-out of solar capacity in ERCOT, the **solar value cannibalisation effect** became severe:
- **Flat average LMP collapsed to $27.44/MWh**
- **Solar capture price cratered to just $21.95/MWh** (capture rate: 80% — solar hours are now *below-average*)
- **Every single month in 2024 saw merchant revenue below the $46/MWh PPA**, with the worst months being February (capture: $8.19/MWh) and March ($11.66/MWh)
- Negative price hours during solar generation rose to **11.6%** of daylight hours

---

### 📉 The Structural Trend: Solar Capture Rate Collapse

| Year | Flat Avg LMP | Solar Capture Price | Capture Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2021 | $145.81 | $78.78 | 54% *(Uri-distorted)* |
| 2022 | $57.49 | $74.69 | **130%** |
| 2023 | $49.47 | $73.59 | **149%** |
| 2024 | $27.44 | $21.95 | **80%** |

From 2022-2023, solar was capturing **a premium** over the flat price — the market had yet to overbuild. By 2024, this completely reversed: solar now earns **below the flat average** price, a direct consequence of ERCOT's explosive solar growth depressing midday prices precisely when solar plants generate.

---

### 💡 Bottom Line for Project Developers & Offtakers

| Scenario | Verdict |
|---|---|
| **2021 merchant vs. $28 PPA** | Merchant wins big — but only because of URI freak event |
| **2022 merchant vs. $40 PPA** | Merchant wins comfortably — strong post-Ukraine gas premium |
| **2023 merchant vs. $45 PPA** | Merchant still wins, but the PPA was being appropriately priced |
| **2024 merchant vs. $46 PPA** | **PPA wins by $21.55/MWh** — merchant underperforms by $47K/MW/year |

> **The $46/MWh 2024 PPA looks excellent in hindsight.** Any developer who locked in $46/MWh in 2024 (or late 2023) would have captured nearly **double** what the merchant market paid. This underscores a broader industry lesson: ERCOT solar PPAs signed at $40–46/MWh in 2022–2024 gave offtakers a very favorable hedge as solar cannibalisation accelerated — and positioned developers to securitize cash flows rather than face the growing Chapter 11 risk of uncontracted ERCOT solar in 2024-2025.

---
*Sources: ERCOT West Hub real-time LMP (`ercot_db.ercot_lmp`); ERCOT system solar generation (`ercot_db.ercot_fuel_mix`); Renewables.ninja solar profile for Midland TX (lat 32.0°, lon –102.1°); PPA prices and REC price per user input.*

steps.reasoning:
Now I have the key info:
- ERCOT has a West hub zone
- LMP data is in ercot_lmp with zone_name and is_day_ahead
- The data appears to be real-time (15-min intervals)
- Midland, TX coordinates: lat=31.9973662, lon=-102.077948

I need to:
1. Get West hub real-time LMP data for 2021-2024 (hourly averages)
2. Get solar generation profiles from Renewables.ninja for 2021-2024 for ERCOT West (Midland, TX)
3. Use the solar generation profile as weights to calculate solar-weighted average prices
4. Compare with PPA prices

Let me first check what zone types are available in the LMP table and verify the data range for 2021-2024.

tool_input.query:
SELECT DISTINCT zone_name, zone_type 
FROM ercot_lmp 
ORDER BY zone_type, zone_name

SELECT MIN(timestamp), MAX(timestamp), COUNT(*) 
FROM ercot_lmp 
WHERE zone_name ILIKE '%west%' AND is_day_ahead = false

steps.content: Good. Now let me fetch the solar profiles for 2022, 2023, 2024, and query the ERCOT West hub real-time LMP data for all 4 years.
steps.reasoning:
Good. Now I need to:
1. Get ERCOT West hub LMP data for 2021-2024 (hourly real-time)
2. Get solar profiles for 2022, 2023, 2024 as well
3. Compute the capacity-weighted revenue per year

The LMP data seems to be 15-minute resolution. I need to aggregate to hourly and then weight by solar production.

Let me get the annual average LMP for West hub in each year, but I also need the hourly profile to weight by solar capacity factor properly.
So my approach is to calculate the solar-weighted average price for each year by taking the sum of hourly LMP multiplied by capacity factor, divided by the total capacity factor. Then I can multiply that by the annual generation to get merchant revenue, and compare it against what a PPA would have paid. Since the LMP data comes in 15-minute intervals, I'll need to aggregate those up to hourly values first, then pull the annual data by year for the West hub.

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM timestamp) AS year,
  date_trunc('hour', timestamp) AS hour,
  AVG(lmp) AS avg_lmp
FROM ercot_lmp
WHERE zone_name = 'west'
  AND zone_type = 'hub'
  AND is_day_ahead = false
  AND timestamp >= '2021-01-01'
  AND timestamp < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY year, date_trunc('hour', timestamp)
ORDER BY hour

steps.content: Now let me get the ERCOT system-wide solar generation hourly data to derive the solar capture rate profile, and then run all the calculations.

tool_input.query:
SELECT 
  EXTRACT(YEAR FROM timestamp) AS year,
  date_trunc('hour', timestamp) AS hour,
  AVG(generation_mw) AS solar_gen_mw
FROM ercot_fuel_mix
WHERE fuel_category = 'solar'
  AND is_day_ahead = false
  AND timestamp >= '2021-01-01'
  AND timestamp < '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY year, date_trunc('hour', timestamp)
ORDER BY hour

steps.reasoning:
Now I have all the data I need. Let me run the comprehensive Python analysis combining:
1. ERCOT West hub real-time LMP data (2021-2024)
2. Solar generation profiles from Renewables.ninja for Midland TX (ERCOT West)
3. ERCOT system-wide solar fuel mix data

Key approach:
- The question says "solar production is consistent with overall ERCOT solar production profile"
- This means I should use the ERCOT solar fuel mix as the shape/profile, normalized to capacity factor
- The Renewables.ninja solar profile gives me actual hourly CF data for West Texas
- I'm interpreting this to mean I should apply the Renewables.ninja profile for ERCOT West as a typical solar asset without curtailment adjustments, since that aligns with the overall ERCOT solar production pattern. I'll use the Renewables.ninja hourly capacity factors for the Midland, TX location in ERCOT West, multiply by a reference capacity to get generation values, and then calculate merchant revenue by multiplying each hour's generation by the corresponding LMP. This gives me the capture rate—the effective value per MWh generated accounting for the solar profile's correlation with price movements throughout the day.

Now I need to align the solar profiles and LMP data by timezone. The Renewables.ninja data comes in UTC, while the ERCOT LMP data is in Central Time. Let me process this carefully in Python to ensure the hourly matching is correct.

Writing Python code... For Renewables.ninja data, I need to convert from UTC to Central time, accounting for daylight saving time—UTC-5 during summer months and UTC-6 in winter. Since the ERCOT timestamps are already in local Central time, I'll align the Renewables.ninja UTC data to match that format.

I'm going to write a Python script that loads solar profiles from 2021-2024, pulls the ERCOT West LMP data, converts the timestamps appropriately, merges everything by hour, and calculates merchant revenues. Though I'm reconsidering whether to use the Renewables.ninja data for Midland or the system-wide ERCOT fuel mix—the Renewables.ninja data is more specific to ERCOT West, which better matches the user's focus on West Texas solar assets.

For calculating the value factor, I'll normalize the ERCOT solar production profile and compute merchant energy revenue per MWh by multiplying hourly LMPs by production levels.

I'll use the Renewables.ninja data for Midland, TX as the production profile since the question specifies ERCOT West and asks for consistency with the overall ERCOT solar pattern without adjustments. I can cross-check this against the ERCOT fuel mix data to validate the approach.

Now I'm setting up the Python analysis to compute annual solar generation, capture rates, and revenue metrics. = Merchant - PPA revenue. Now I'm writing Python code to handle the data processing, starting with converting the Renewables.ninja UTC timestamps in milliseconds to Central time so they align with the ERCOT LMP data, which uses local Central time with DST adjustments.

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# ── 1. Load ERCOT West hub hourly LMP data ──────────────────────────────────
lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053148.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
lmp = lmp.rename(columns={'hour': 'timestamp_local', 'avg_lmp': 'lmp'})
lmp['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(lmp['timestamp_local'])
print(f"LMP rows: {len(lmp)} — range: {lmp['timestamp_local'].min()} to {lmp['timestamp_local'].max()}")
print(lmp.head(3))

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# ── 1. Load LMP ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053148.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
lmp = lmp.rename(columns={'hour': 'timestamp_local', 'avg_lmp': 'lmp'})
lmp['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(lmp['timestamp_local'])

# ── 2. Load ERCOT system-wide solar generation ───────────────────────────────
solar_sys = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053207.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
solar_sys = solar_sys.rename(columns={'hour': 'timestamp_local', 'solar_gen_mw': 'solar_mw'})
solar_sys['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_sys['timestamp_local'])

# Normalize system solar to capacity factor (0-1) per year
for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    mask = solar_sys['year'] == yr
    max_val = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'].max()
    solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_cf'] = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'] / max_val

print("System solar CF sample:")
print(solar_sys[solar_sys['solar_mw'] > 0].head(5)[['timestamp_local','solar_mw','solar_cf']])

# ── 3. Load Renewables.ninja solar profiles for Midland, TX ─────────────────
# Files per year: 2021, 2022, 2023, 2024
solar_files = {
    2021: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053123.csv',
    2022: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053141.csv',
    2023: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053144.csv',
    2024: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053146.csv',
}

ninja_dfs = []
for yr, fpath in solar_files.items():
    df = pd.read_csv(fpath)
    print(f"\nYear {yr} columns: {list(df.columns)}")
    print(df.head(3))
    break  # Check structure first

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import ast

# ── Helper to load Renewables.ninja solar CSV ────────────────────────────────
def load_ninja_solar(fpath):
    df = pd.read_csv(fpath)
    df['timestamp_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'].astype(float), unit='ms', utc=True)
    # timestamp is UTC — convert to US Central (CST = UTC-6 standard, CDT = UTC-5)
    df['timestamp_local'] = df['timestamp_utc'].dt.tz_convert('America/Chicago').dt.tz_localize(None)
    # parse the electricity value
    def parse_val(v):
        if isinstance(v, float): return v
        try:
            d = ast.literal_eval(str(v))
            return d['electricity']
        except:
            return float(str(v).replace("{'electricity': ", '').replace('}','').strip())
    df['solar_cf'] = df['value'].apply(parse_val)
    return df[['timestamp_local','solar_cf']].copy()

solar_files = {
    2021: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053123.csv',
    2022: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053141.csv',
    2023: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053144.csv',
    2024: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053146.csv',
}

ninja_dfs = []
for yr, fpath in solar_files.items():
    df = load_ninja_solar(fpath)
    df['year'] = yr
    # Filter to correct calendar year (Central time)
    df = df[(df['timestamp_local'] >= f'{yr}-01-01') & (df['timestamp_local'] < f'{yr+1}-01-01')]
    ninja_dfs.append(df)
    
ninja = pd.concat(ninja_dfs, ignore_index=True)
print(f"Ninja solar rows: {len(ninja)}")
print(ninja[ninja['solar_cf']>0].head(5))
print("\nAnnual CFs from Ninja:")
for yr in [2021,2022,2023,2024]:
    subset = ninja[ninja['year']==yr]
    print(f"  {yr}: {subset['solar_cf'].mean():.4f} ({len(subset)} hours)")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import ast

# ── Helper ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def load_ninja_solar(fpath):
    df = pd.read_csv(fpath)
    df['timestamp_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'].astype(float), unit='ms', utc=True)
    df['timestamp_local'] = df['timestamp_utc'].dt.tz_convert('America/Chicago').dt.tz_localize(None)
    def parse_val(v):
        if isinstance(v, float): return v
        try:
            d = ast.literal_eval(str(v))
            return d['electricity']
        except:
            return float(str(v).replace("{'electricity': ", '').replace('}','').strip())
    df['solar_cf'] = df['value'].apply(parse_val)
    return df[['timestamp_local','solar_cf']].copy()

solar_files = {
    2021: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053123.csv',
    2022: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053141.csv',
    2023: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053144.csv',
    2024: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053146.csv',
}

# ── Load data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ninja_dfs = []
for yr, fpath in solar_files.items():
    df = load_ninja_solar(fpath)
    df['year'] = yr
    df = df[(df['timestamp_local'] >= f'{yr}-01-01') & (df['timestamp_local'] < f'{yr+1}-01-01')]
    ninja_dfs.append(df)
ninja = pd.concat(ninja_dfs, ignore_index=True)

lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053148.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
lmp['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(lmp['hour'])
lmp = lmp.rename(columns={'avg_lmp': 'lmp'})

solar_sys = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053207.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
solar_sys['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_sys['hour'])
solar_sys = solar_sys.rename(columns={'solar_gen_mw': 'solar_mw'})

# Normalize system solar to CF per year  
for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    mask = solar_sys['year'] == yr
    max_val = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'].max()
    if max_val > 0:
        solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_cf_sys'] = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'] / max_val

# ── Merge all three on timestamp + year ──────────────────────────────────────
# Align timestamps carefully: LMP data is in CST (no DST)
# Ninja is converted to America/Chicago (CDT/CST)

merged = ninja.merge(lmp[['timestamp_local','lmp']], on='timestamp_local', how='inner')
merged = merged.merge(solar_sys[['timestamp_local','solar_cf_sys']], on='timestamp_local', how='left')

print(f"Merged rows: {len(merged)}")
print(merged[merged['solar_cf']>0].head(5)[['timestamp_local','solar_cf','solar_cf_sys','lmp']])

# ── Option A: Use Renewables.ninja (Midland) as wind-weighted approach
# ── Option B: Use ERCOT system solar CF profile (consistent with ERCOT solar)
# Question says "consistent with overall ERCOT solar production profile"
# → Use system solar CF normalized profile

# Per user instruction: use ERCOT system solar production profile
# We'll use solar_cf_sys as the capacity factor

merged['solar_cf_used'] = merged['solar_cf_sys']

# Fill any small NaN for nighttime (system solar is near 0)
merged['solar_cf_used'] = merged['solar_cf_used'].fillna(0)

# ── Merchant revenue calculation ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Merchant revenue = CF * LMP  (per MWh of installed capacity, per hour → summed = $/MW-year)
# But we want $/MWh of actual generation = sum(CF * LMP) / sum(CF)
# This is the "capture price" = energy-weighted average price

results = []
for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    df_yr = merged[merged['year'] == yr].copy()
    
    # Daytime hours only (solar generation > 0)
    df_sun = df_yr[df_yr['solar_cf_used'] > 0.001].copy()
    
    total_hours = len(df_yr)
    sun_hours   = len(df_sun)
    
    # Production-weighted (capture) price
    total_gen_cf   = df_yr['solar_cf_used'].sum()          # sum of CF over all hours
    total_rev_cf   = (df_yr['solar_cf_used'] * df_yr['lmp']).sum()  # CF-weighted LMP
    
    capture_price  = total_rev_cf / total_gen_cf if total_gen_cf > 0 else 0
    
    # Flat (unweighted) average LMP
    avg_lmp_all    = df_yr['lmp'].mean()
    avg_lmp_daytime= df_sun['lmp'].mean()  # average during solar hours
    
    # Annual CF
    annual_cf      = df_yr['solar_cf_used'].mean()
    
    # Annual generation (MWh per MW installed)
    annual_gen_mwh = annual_cf * total_hours   # = sum of CF * 1 MW * 1 hr
    
    # Merchant revenue per MWh of generation
    merchant_energy_per_mwh = capture_price
    merchant_total_per_mw   = total_rev_cf  # $/MW installed/year
    
    results.append({
        'year': yr,
        'total_hours': total_hours,
        'daylight_hours': sun_hours,
        'annual_cf_pct': round(annual_cf * 100, 2),
        'annual_gen_mwh_per_mw': round(annual_gen_mwh, 0),
        'avg_lmp_all_hours': round(avg_lmp_all, 2),
        'avg_lmp_daytime': round(avg_lmp_daytime, 2),
        'solar_capture_price': round(capture_price, 2),
        'merchant_energy_per_mwh': round(merchant_energy_per_mwh, 2),
        'merchant_rev_per_mw_yr': round(merchant_total_per_mw, 0),
    })

res = pd.DataFrame(results)
print("\n=== ERCOT West Hub — Solar Production-Weighted (Capture) Results ===")
print(res.to_string(index=False))

steps.reasoning:
The 2021 annual CF of 24.93% seems quite high for a normalized system solar profile approach. Let me investigate the 2021 data more closely, particularly around the February winter storm event which had extreme prices. That's likely inflating the 2021 capture price significantly ... and the daytime average actually looks reasonable ($136/MWh) with the Feb 2021 storm.

Wait, looking at the numbers:
- 2021: capture = $78.78/MWh with annual CF of 24.93% — this is heavily influenced by Feb 2021 storm prices
- 2022 and 2023 show more moderate capture prices around $74-75/MWh, while 2024 drops significantly to $21.95/MWh

The 2021 capture price makes sense, but I'm wondering if the February storm is skewing things — solar generation was probably minimal during that cold winter period, yet LMPs spiked dramatically, which would artificially boost the capture price. Looking at the system-level capacity factors, the 24.93% in 2021 versus 26.57% in 2023 both seem elevated for typical ERCOT solar performance.

These are normalized capacity factors, so they're scaled by the maximum output in each year. The challenge is that ERCOT's total solar capacity was much smaller in 2021 (maybe 2-3 GW) compared to 2024 (10+ GW), which affects how the normalization works. But the production profile shape — which hours generate what fraction of peak output — should remain fairly consistent across years, so using the system-wide normalized profile for this analysis makes sense even if the absolute generation per MW would differ. The normalized approach actually works well here—dividing by the maximum generation gives us a valid capacity factor that reflects how often the system operates near peak output. For West Texas solar in 2021, a 25% capacity factor is reasonable. The key insight is that I'm using the ERCOT system solar generation shape to capture the temporal pattern of when solar produces across the year, then scaling it to realistic capacity factors rather than trying to match the exact system-wide CF.

Now I'm noticing the 2024 capture price of $21.95/MWh is substantially lower than the flat average of $27.44—this reflects the duck curve effect where solar generation peaks during midday when wholesale prices are depressed. Let me work through the full comparison incorporating PPA pricing.

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import ast

# ── Helper ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def load_ninja_solar(fpath):
    df = pd.read_csv(fpath)
    df['timestamp_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'].astype(float), unit='ms', utc=True)
    df['timestamp_local'] = df['timestamp_utc'].dt.tz_convert('America/Chicago').dt.tz_localize(None)
    def parse_val(v):
        if isinstance(v, float): return v
        try:
            d = ast.literal_eval(str(v))
            return d['electricity']
        except:
            return float(str(v).replace("{'electricity': ", '').replace('}','').strip())
    df['solar_cf'] = df['value'].apply(parse_val)
    return df[['timestamp_local','solar_cf']].copy()

solar_files = {
    2021: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053123.csv',
    2022: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053141.csv',
    2023: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053144.csv',
    2024: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053146.csv',
}
ninja_dfs = []
for yr, fpath in solar_files.items():
    df = load_ninja_solar(fpath)
    df['year'] = yr
    df = df[(df['timestamp_local'] >= f'{yr}-01-01') & (df['timestamp_local'] < f'{yr+1}-01-01')]
    ninja_dfs.append(df)
ninja = pd.concat(ninja_dfs, ignore_index=True)

lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053148.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
lmp['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(lmp['hour'])
lmp = lmp.rename(columns={'avg_lmp': 'lmp'})

solar_sys = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053207.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
solar_sys['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_sys['hour'])
solar_sys = solar_sys.rename(columns={'solar_gen_mw': 'solar_mw'})

for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    mask = solar_sys['year'] == yr
    max_val = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'].max()
    if max_val > 0:
        solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_cf_sys'] = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'] / max_val

merged = ninja.merge(lmp[['timestamp_local','lmp']], on='timestamp_local', how='inner')
merged = merged.merge(solar_sys[['timestamp_local','solar_cf_sys']], on='timestamp_local', how='left')
merged['solar_cf_sys'] = merged['solar_cf_sys'].fillna(0)

# ── PPA prices and REC price ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
ppa_prices = {2021: 28.0, 2022: 40.0, 2023: 45.0, 2024: 46.0}
rec_price   = 2.5   # $/MWh

# ── Full comparison ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
results = []
for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    df_yr = merged[merged['year'] == yr].copy()
    
    # ERCOT system solar CF as the production profile
    cf          = df_yr['solar_cf_sys']
    total_cf    = cf.sum()
    total_hours = len(df_yr)
    
    # Annual production per MW installed (MWh) — using the system CF shape
    # Note: "total_cf" = sum of normalized CF × 1 MW × 1 hour = MWh/MW-yr (for the normalized profile)
    # But actual annual gen = CF_annual × 8760
    annual_cf   = cf.mean()
    annual_gen  = annual_cf * total_hours   # MWh per MW installed

    # ----- MERCHANT ENERGY REVENUE -----
    # Revenue per MWh generated = production-weighted (capture) price
    merchant_capture_px = (cf * df_yr['lmp']).sum() / total_cf if total_cf > 0 else 0
    # Plus REC revenue
    merchant_total_px   = merchant_capture_px + rec_price

    # Revenue per MW installed per year ($)
    merchant_rev_per_mw = (cf * df_yr['lmp']).sum() + rec_price * annual_gen

    # ----- PPA REVENUE -----
    ppa_px              = ppa_prices[yr]   # already includes RECs
    ppa_rev_per_mw      = ppa_px * annual_gen

    # ----- DELTA -----
    delta_per_mwh       = merchant_total_px   - ppa_px
    delta_per_mw        = merchant_rev_per_mw - ppa_rev_per_mw

    # Key context metrics
    flat_avg_lmp  = df_yr['lmp'].mean()
    day_lmp       = df_yr.loc[cf > 0.001, 'lmp'].mean()

    results.append({
        'year'                     : yr,
        'annual_cf_pct'            : round(annual_cf * 100, 1),
        'annual_gen_mwh_per_mw'    : round(annual_gen, 0),
        'flat_avg_lmp'             : round(flat_avg_lmp, 2),
        'solar_daytime_avg_lmp'    : round(day_lmp, 2),
        'solar_capture_price'      : round(merchant_capture_px, 2),
        'merchant_energy_px'       : round(merchant_capture_px, 2),
        'rec_price'                : rec_price,
        'merchant_total_px'        : round(merchant_total_px, 2),
        'ppa_px_incl_rec'          : ppa_px,
        'delta_px_merchant_vs_ppa' : round(delta_per_mwh, 2),
        'delta_rev_per_mw_yr'      : round(delta_per_mw, 0),
        'merchant_rev_per_mw_yr'   : round(merchant_rev_per_mw, 0),
        'ppa_rev_per_mw_yr'        : round(ppa_rev_per_mw, 0),
    })

res = pd.DataFrame(results)
print(res.to_string(index=False))

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import ast

# ── Re-load everything ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def load_ninja_solar(fpath):
    df = pd.read_csv(fpath)
    df['timestamp_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'].astype(float), unit='ms', utc=True)
    df['timestamp_local'] = df['timestamp_utc'].dt.tz_convert('America/Chicago').dt.tz_localize(None)
    def parse_val(v):
        if isinstance(v, float): return v
        try:
            d = ast.literal_eval(str(v))
            return d['electricity']
        except:
            return float(str(v).replace("{'electricity': ", '').replace('}','').strip())
    df['solar_cf'] = df['value'].apply(parse_val)
    return df[['timestamp_local','solar_cf']].copy()

solar_files = {
    2021: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053123.csv',
    2022: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053141.csv',
    2023: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053144.csv',
    2024: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053146.csv',
}
ninja_dfs = []
for yr, fpath in solar_files.items():
    df = load_ninja_solar(fpath)
    df['year'] = yr
    df = df[(df['timestamp_local'] >= f'{yr}-01-01') & (df['timestamp_local'] < f'{yr+1}-01-01')]
    ninja_dfs.append(df)
ninja = pd.concat(ninja_dfs, ignore_index=True)

lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053148.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
lmp['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(lmp['hour'])
lmp = lmp.rename(columns={'avg_lmp': 'lmp'})

solar_sys = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053207.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
solar_sys['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_sys['hour'])
solar_sys = solar_sys.rename(columns={'solar_gen_mw': 'solar_mw'})

for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    mask = solar_sys['year'] == yr
    max_val = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'].max()
    if max_val > 0:
        solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_cf_sys'] = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'] / max_val

merged = ninja.merge(lmp[['timestamp_local','lmp']], on='timestamp_local', how='inner')
merged = merged.merge(solar_sys[['timestamp_local','solar_cf_sys']], on='timestamp_local', how='left')
merged['solar_cf_sys'] = merged['solar_cf_sys'].fillna(0)

ppa_prices = {2021: 28.0, 2022: 40.0, 2023: 45.0, 2024: 46.0}
rec_price   = 2.5

# ─── DEEP DIVE: Year-by-year analysis ─────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 90)
print("  ERCOT WEST HUB — SOLAR MERCHANT vs. PPA REVENUE ANALYSIS (2021–2024)")
print("  Solar Profile: ERCOT System-wide Solar (normalized CF) | Location: Midland, TX")
print("  REC price: $2.50/MWh | PPA prices include RECs")
print("=" * 90)

all_results = []
for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    df_yr  = merged[merged['year'] == yr].copy()
    cf     = df_yr['solar_cf_sys']
    lmp_hr = df_yr['lmp']
    total_cf   = cf.sum()
    total_hours= len(df_yr)
    annual_cf  = cf.mean()
    annual_gen = annual_cf * total_hours   # MWh per MW

    # Merchant breakdown
    capture_px  = (cf * lmp_hr).sum() / total_cf if total_cf > 0 else 0
    total_merch = capture_px + rec_price

    # PPA
    ppa_px      = ppa_prices[yr]
    delta_px    = total_merch - ppa_px

    merchant_rev_per_mw = (cf * lmp_hr).sum() + rec_price * annual_gen
    ppa_rev_per_mw      = ppa_px * annual_gen
    delta_rev_per_mw    = merchant_rev_per_mw - ppa_rev_per_mw

    # Percentile analysis of captured LMP
    lmp_during_solar = lmp_hr[cf > 0.01]
    p10 = lmp_during_solar.quantile(0.10)
    p50 = lmp_during_solar.quantile(0.50)
    p90 = lmp_during_solar.quantile(0.90)
    neg_pct = (lmp_hr[cf > 0.01] < 0).sum() / max((cf > 0.01).sum(), 1) * 100

    all_results.append({
        'year': yr, 'annual_cf_pct': round(annual_cf*100,1),
        'annual_gen_mwh_per_mw': round(annual_gen,0),
        'flat_avg_lmp': round(lmp_hr.mean(),2),
        'capture_price': round(capture_px,2),
        'rec_price': rec_price,
        'merchant_total_px': round(total_merch,2),
        'ppa_px': ppa_px,
        'delta_px': round(delta_px,2),
        'merchant_rev_mw_yr': round(merchant_rev_per_mw,0),
        'ppa_rev_mw_yr': round(ppa_rev_per_mw,0),
        'delta_rev_mw_yr': round(delta_rev_per_mw,0),
        'neg_price_pct_daytime': round(neg_pct,1),
        'p10_lmp': round(p10,1), 'p50_lmp': round(p50,1), 'p90_lmp': round(p90,1),
    })

df_out = pd.DataFrame(all_results)

# ─── Table 1: Core Price Comparison ───────────────────────────────────────────
print("\n📊 TABLE 1: REVENUE PER MWh GENERATED ($/MWh)")
print("-" * 75)
print(f"{'Metric':<40} {'2021':>8} {'2022':>8} {'2023':>8} {'2024':>8}")
print("-" * 75)
rows_t1 = [
    ('Solar Capture Price (energy only)',      'capture_price'),
    ('+ REC Revenue',                          'rec_price'),
    ('= Merchant Total (energy + RECs)',        'merchant_total_px'),
    ('',                                       None),
    ('PPA Price (all-in, incl. RECs)',          'ppa_px'),
    ('',                                       None),
    ('Delta: Merchant MINUS PPA ($/MWh)',       'delta_px'),
]
for label, col in rows_t1:
    if col is None:
        print()
        continue
    vals = [df_out.loc[df_out['year']==yr, col].values[0] for yr in [2021,2022,2023,2024]]
    stars = ['   ✅' if v > 0 else '   ❌' if v < 0 else '' if label == '' else '' for v in vals]
    if col == 'delta_px':
        print(f"{'  ' + label:<40} {'':>8} {'':>8} {'':>8} {'':>8}")
        for i, (v, s) in enumerate(zip(vals, stars)):
            if i == 0:
                print(f"  {'  ' + label:<38} {v:>+8.2f}{s}", end='')
            else:
                print(f" {v:>+8.2f}{s}", end='')
        print()
    else:
        row = f"  {label:<38}"
        for v in vals:
            if isinstance(v, float):
                row += f" {v:>8.2f}"
            else:
                row += f" {v:>8}"
        print(row)

# ─── Table 2: Annual Revenue per MW Installed ─────────────────────────────────
print("\n\n📊 TABLE 2: ANNUAL REVENUE PER MW INSTALLED ($/MW/year)")
print("-" * 75)
print(f"{'Metric':<40} {'2021':>8} {'2022':>8} {'2023':>8} {'2024':>8}")
print("-" * 75)
rows_t2 = [
    ('Annual Generation (MWh/MW)',              'annual_gen_mwh_per_mw'),
    ('Annual CF (%)',                            'annual_cf_pct'),
    ('Flat Average LMP ($/MWh)',                'flat_avg_lmp'),
    ('',                                        None),
    ('Merchant Revenue (energy + RECs)',         'merchant_rev_mw_yr'),
    ('PPA Revenue (incl. RECs)',                 'ppa_rev_mw_yr'),
    ('Delta: Merchant MINUS PPA',               'delta_rev_mw_yr'),
]
for label, col in rows_t2:
    if col is None:
        print(); continue
    vals = [df_out.loc[df_out['year']==yr, col].values[0] for yr in [2021,2022,2023,2024]]
    row = f"  {label:<38}"
    for v in vals:
        if label.startswith('Delta'):
            row += f" {v:>+8,.0f}"
        elif isinstance(v, float) and v > 100:
            row += f" {v:>8,.0f}"
        else:
            row += f" {v:>8.1f}"
    print(row)

# ─── Table 3: LMP Statistics During Solar Hours ──────────────────────────────
print("\n\n📊 TABLE 3: LMP DISTRIBUTION DURING SOLAR GENERATION HOURS")
print("-" * 75)
print(f"{'Metric':<40} {'2021':>8} {'2022':>8} {'2023':>8} {'2024':>8}")
print("-" * 75)
rows_t3 = [
    ('Daytime P10 LMP ($/MWh)',      'p10_lmp'),
    ('Daytime P50 LMP ($/MWh)',      'p50_lmp'),
    ('Daytime P90 LMP ($/MWh)',      'p90_lmp'),
    ('Negative Price Hours (%)',     'neg_price_pct_daytime'),
]
for label, col in rows_t3:
    vals = [df_out.loc[df_out['year']==yr, col].values[0] for yr in [2021,2022,2023,2024]]
    row = f"  {label:<38}"
    for v in vals:
        row += f" {v:>8.1f}"
    print(row)

print("\n")
print("Sources: ERCOT West Hub RT-LMP (ercot_db), ERCOT System Solar Generation (ercot_db),")
print("         Renewables.ninja solar profile (Midland, TX 32°N, 102°W), tilt 25°, azimuth 180°")
print("         PPA prices provided by user. REC price assumption: $2.50/MWh.")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import ast

# ── Re-load ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def load_ninja_solar(fpath):
    df = pd.read_csv(fpath)
    df['timestamp_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'].astype(float), unit='ms', utc=True)
    df['timestamp_local'] = df['timestamp_utc'].dt.tz_convert('America/Chicago').dt.tz_localize(None)
    def parse_val(v):
        if isinstance(v, float): return v
        try:
            d = ast.literal_eval(str(v))
            return d['electricity']
        except:
            return float(str(v).replace("{'electricity': ", '').replace('}','').strip())
    df['solar_cf'] = df['value'].apply(parse_val)
    return df[['timestamp_local','solar_cf']].copy()

solar_files = {
    2021: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053123.csv',
    2022: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053141.csv',
    2023: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053144.csv',
    2024: '/work/solar_profile_20260614_053146.csv',
}
ninja_dfs = []
for yr, fpath in solar_files.items():
    df = load_ninja_solar(fpath)
    df['year'] = yr
    df = df[(df['timestamp_local'] >= f'{yr}-01-01') & (df['timestamp_local'] < f'{yr+1}-01-01')]
    ninja_dfs.append(df)
ninja = pd.concat(ninja_dfs, ignore_index=True)

lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053148.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
lmp['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(lmp['hour'])
lmp = lmp.rename(columns={'avg_lmp': 'lmp'})

solar_sys = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260614_053207.csv', parse_dates=['hour'])
solar_sys['timestamp_local'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_sys['hour'])
solar_sys = solar_sys.rename(columns={'solar_gen_mw': 'solar_mw'})

for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    mask = solar_sys['year'] == yr
    max_val = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'].max()
    if max_val > 0:
        solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_cf_sys'] = solar_sys.loc[mask, 'solar_mw'] / max_val

merged = ninja.merge(lmp[['timestamp_local','lmp']], on='timestamp_local', how='inner')
merged = merged.merge(solar_sys[['timestamp_local','solar_cf_sys']], on='timestamp_local', how='left')
merged['solar_cf_sys'] = merged['solar_cf_sys'].fillna(0)

ppa_prices = {2021: 28.0, 2022: 40.0, 2023: 45.0, 2024: 46.0}
rec_price   = 2.5

# ─── Capture Rate analysis ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Capture rate = solar capture price / flat average LMP
print("=== CAPTURE RATE ANALYSIS (solar capture price / flat avg LMP) ===\n")
for yr in [2021, 2022, 2023, 2024]:
    df_yr = merged[merged['year'] == yr].copy()
    cf    = df_yr['solar_cf_sys']
    total_cf = cf.sum()
    lmp_flat = df_yr['lmp'].mean()
    capture  = (cf * df_yr['lmp']).sum() / total_cf if total_cf > 0 else 0
    print(f"  {yr}: Flat avg LMP = ${lmp_flat:.2f}/MWh  |  Capture = ${capture:.2f}/MWh  |  Capture Rate = {capture/lmp_flat*100:.1f}%")

# ─── Hourly Revenue Waterfall ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("\n\n=== MONTHLY AVERAGE SOLAR CAPTURE PRICES — 2024 (The Canary Year) ===\n")
df_24 = merged[merged['year'] == 2024].copy()
df_24['month'] = pd.to_datetime(df_24['timestamp_local']).dt.month
months = {1:'Jan',2:'Feb',3:'Mar',4:'Apr',5:'May',6:'Jun',7:'Jul',8:'Aug',9:'Sep',10:'Oct',11:'Nov',12:'Dec'}

for mo in range(1, 13):
    sub = df_24[df_24['month'] == mo]
    cf  = sub['solar_cf_sys']
    tf  = cf.sum()
    if tf > 0:
        cap = (cf * sub['lmp']).sum() / tf
        flat= sub['lmp'].mean()
        neg = (sub.loc[cf>0.01, 'lmp'] < 0).sum()
        total_sun = (cf > 0.01).sum()
        gen  = cf.sum()   # MWh per MW installed
        merchant_mo = cap + rec_price
        ppa_mo = ppa_prices[2024]
        delta_mo = merchant_mo - ppa_mo
        bar = '█' * int(max(0, cap/5)) + ('▓' * int(max(0, -cap/5)) if cap < 0 else '')
        print(f"  {months[mo]:>3}: capture=${cap:>6.2f}/MWh  flat_lmp=${flat:>6.2f}  neg_hrs={neg:>3}/{total_sun}  merch={merchant_mo:>6.2f}  ppa=46.00  delta={delta_mo:>+7.2f}")

# ─── February 2021 (winter storm) check ─────────────────────────────────────
print("\n\n=== FEBRUARY 2021 WINTER STORM URI WEEK (Feb 10-20) ===\n")
df_uri = merged[(merged['year']==2021) & 
                (pd.to_datetime(merged['timestamp_local']).dt.month == 2) &
                (pd.to_datetime(merged['timestamp_local']).dt.day.between(10,20))].copy()
cf_uri = df_uri['solar_cf_sys']
tf_uri = cf_uri.sum()
cap_uri = (cf_uri * df_uri['lmp']).sum() / tf_uri if tf_uri > 0 else 0
print(f"  Feb 10-20, 2021: avg LMP = ${df_uri['lmp'].mean():.2f}/MWh | solar capture price = ${cap_uri:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  Max LMP during this period: ${df_uri['lmp'].max():.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  Note: URI drove 2021 flat average LMP dramatically above normal.\n")
# Compare without extreme days
df_ex = merged[(merged['year']==2021) & 
               ~((pd.to_datetime(merged['timestamp_local']).dt.month == 2) &
                 (pd.to_datetime(merged['timestamp_local']).dt.day.between(10,20)))]
cf_ex = df_ex['solar_cf_sys']
tf_ex = cf_ex.sum()
cap_ex = (cf_ex * df_ex['lmp']).sum() / tf_ex if tf_ex > 0 else 0
flat_ex= df_ex['lmp'].mean()
print(f"  2021 excl. URI week: flat avg LMP = ${flat_ex:.2f}/MWh | solar capture = ${cap_ex:.2f}/MWh")

steps.content:
Excellent! Now I have everything I need to write the full, rich analysis. Let me compile the final narrative.

---

## ERCOT West Solar: Merchant Revenue vs. PPA Revenue (2021–2024)

**Methodology & Assumptions:**
- **Solar profile**: ERCOT system-wide real-time solar generation (from `ercot_fuel_mix`, normalized to a 0–1 capacity factor shape) — consistent with "overall ERCOT solar production profile" as requested
- **Energy prices**: ERCOT West Hub real-time LMP (15-min data, hourly-averaged) from `ercot_lmp`
- **Solar location reference**: Midland, TX (31.997°N, 102.078°W) via Renewables.ninja to confirm annual CFs (~25-27%)
- **Merchant revenue** = solar capture price (production-weighted LMP) + $2.50/MWh RECs
- **PPA revenue** = given fixed PPA $/MWh (already inclusive of RECs)
- No curtailment assumed; no degradation charges applied

---

### 📊 TABLE 1: Revenue Per MWh of Generation ($/MWh)

| Metric | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solar Capture Price** (energy only) | $78.78 | $74.69 | $73.59 | $21.95 |
| + REC Revenue | $2.50 | $2.50 | $2.50 | $2.50 |
| **= Merchant Total (energy + RECs)** | **$81.28** | **$77.19** | **$76.09** | **$24.45** |
| | | | | |
| **PPA Price (all-in, incl. RECs)** | **$28.00** | **$40.00** | **$45.00** | **$46.00** |
| | | | | |
| 🔼/🔽 **Delta: Merchant MINUS PPA** | **+$53.28 ✅** | **+$37.19 ✅** | **+$31.09 ✅** | **−$21.55 ❌** |

---

### 📊 TABLE 2: Annual Revenue Per MW Installed ($/MW/year)

| Metric | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Annual CF (%) | 24.9% | 26.9% | 26.6% | 25.1% |
| Annual Generation (MWh/MW) | 2,182 | 2,358 | 2,326 | 2,201 |
| Flat Average ERCOT West LMP ($/MWh) | $145.81 | $57.49 | $49.47 | $27.44 |
| | | | | |
| **Merchant Revenue** (energy + RECs) | **$177,366** | **$182,013** | **$176,977** | **$53,807** |
| **PPA Revenue** (incl. RECs) | $61,102 | $94,322 | $104,666 | $101,249 |
| **Delta: Merchant MINUS PPA** | **+$116,264 ✅** | **+$87,692 ✅** | **+$72,311 ✅** | **−$47,442 ❌** |

---

### 📊 TABLE 3: LMP Distribution During Solar Hours (ERCOT West Hub)

| Metric | 2021 | 2022 | 2023 | 2024 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Daytime P10 LMP ($/MWh) | $0.8 | –$0.3 | $0.2 | –$0.3 |
| Daytime P50 LMP ($/MWh) | $26.1 | $43.5 | $21.7 | $18.2 |
| Daytime P90 LMP ($/MWh) | $63.5 | $96.8 | $74.4 | $51.5 |
| Negative-price hours (daytime) | 7.6% | 11.1% | 8.8% | 11.6% |

---

### 🔍 Key Findings & Drivers

#### 1. 🏆 2021 — Massive Merchant Outperformance (+$53/MWh; +$116K/MW)
The **2021 flat average ERCOT West LMP was $145.81/MWh** — the highest in any year — almost entirely due to **Winter Storm Uri (Feb 10-20)**, which drove prices to $9,199/MWh and a 10-day average of $3,868/MWh. Solar plants were running during some Uri daylight hours and captured an average of $3,785/MWh during that period. Even excluding Uri, 2021 solar capture (~$37.58/MWh) still well exceeded the $28 PPA.

> ⚠️ **Important caveat**: The outsized 2021 merchant figure is a **once-in-a-decade event** — a merchant investor capturing these prices in real life would need to have stayed unhedged through Uri. In practice, many contracts and prudency standards may prevent this.

#### 2. ✅ 2022 — Strong Outperformance (+$37/MWh; +$88K/MW) with Counterintuitive Capture
Despite the flat average LMP dropping from $146 to $57, the solar capture rate jumped to **129.9%** (capture price higher than flat average), because the Russia/Ukraine energy crisis pushed gas and power prices up disproportionately during peak demand hours that overlap with solar production. The P90 daytime LMP was $96.8/MWh.

#### 3. ✅ 2023 — Continued Outperformance (+$31/MWh; +$72K/MW), But Narrowing Gap
Capture price remained elevated at $73.59/MWh against a flat average of $49.47/MWh (capture rate: **149%**!). Solar was quite well-correlated with peak prices this year. However, the PPA price of $45 was creeping up toward the capture price, signaling the market was beginning to fairly price long-run solar value.

#### 4. ❌ 2024 — Merchant Cracks Under Solar Cannibalisation (−$21.55/MWh; −$47K/MW)
This is **the canary in the coal mine for Texas solar.** With massive build-out of solar capacity in ERCOT, the **solar value cannibalisation effect** became severe:
- **Flat average LMP collapsed to $27.44/MWh**
- **Solar capture price cratered to just $21.95/MWh** (capture rate: 80% — solar hours are now *below-average*)
- **Every single month in 2024 saw merchant revenue below the $46/MWh PPA**, with the worst months being February (capture: $8.19/MWh) and March ($11.66/MWh)
- Negative price hours during solar generation rose to **11.6%** of daylight hours

---

### 📉 The Structural Trend: Solar Capture Rate Collapse

| Year | Flat Avg LMP | Solar Capture Price | Capture Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2021 | $145.81 | $78.78 | 54% *(Uri-distorted)* |
| 2022 | $57.49 | $74.69 | **130%** |
| 2023 | $49.47 | $73.59 | **149%** |
| 2024 | $27.44 | $21.95 | **80%** |

From 2022-2023, solar was capturing **a premium** over the flat price — the market had yet to overbuild. By 2024, this completely reversed: solar now earns **below the flat average** price, a direct consequence of ERCOT's explosive solar growth depressing midday prices precisely when solar plants generate.

---

### 💡 Bottom Line for Project Developers & Offtakers

| Scenario | Verdict |
|---|---|
| **2021 merchant vs. $28 PPA** | Merchant wins big — but only because of URI freak event |
| **2022 merchant vs. $40 PPA** | Merchant wins comfortably — strong post-Ukraine gas premium |
| **2023 merchant vs. $45 PPA** | Merchant still wins, but the PPA was being appropriately priced |
| **2024 merchant vs. $46 PPA** | **PPA wins by $21.55/MWh** — merchant underperforms by $47K/MW/year |

> **The $46/MWh 2024 PPA looks excellent in hindsight.** Any developer who locked in $46/MWh in 2024 (or late 2023) would have captured nearly **double** what the merchant market paid. This underscores a broader industry lesson: ERCOT solar PPAs signed at $40–46/MWh in 2022–2024 gave offtakers a very favorable hedge as solar cannibalisation accelerated — and positioned developers to securitize cash flows rather than face the growing Chapter 11 risk of uncontracted ERCOT solar in 2024-2025.

---
*Sources: ERCOT West Hub real-time LMP (`ercot_db.ercot_lmp`); ERCOT system solar generation (`ercot_db.ercot_fuel_mix`); Renewables.ninja solar profile for Midland TX (lat 32.0°, lon –102.1°); PPA prices and REC price per user input.*
